title: DGCA Orders Fleet-Wide Safety Audit After MetroAir Engine Incident; 86 Flights Delayed
content:
India's aviation regulator DGCA has ordered an immediate fleet-wide technical audit of MetroAir's narrow-body aircraft after a mid-air engine vibration alert forced a precautionary return of flight MA-219 from Delhi to Bengaluru on Friday morning. The aircraft landed safely and no injuries were reported.

The regulator has directed MetroAir to complete borescope checks, oil system validation, and vibration trend analysis across 52 aircraft within 72 hours. Until the checks are completed, the airline has been asked to operate with enhanced maintenance release protocols and additional line checks before departure.

The incident disrupted operations at three major hubs. Airport officials said at least 86 flights were delayed and 14 were cancelled by late afternoon, affecting an estimated 19,000 passengers. MetroAir said it was offering full refunds and one-time free rescheduling to impacted travellers.

A preliminary engineering note reviewed by officials indicates the cockpit received repeated vibration exceedance alerts in the climb phase, prompting pilots to follow standard safety procedures and return to origin. The airline said the crew response was "as per checklist" and the aircraft has been grounded pending investigation.

Civil Aviation Minister Jyotiraditya Scindia said passenger safety remains non-negotiable and promised transparent disclosure of findings. The ministry has asked all scheduled carriers to submit incident trend dashboards for the last six months by next week.

Aviation analysts said the event may lead to tighter predictive maintenance norms and higher short-term costs for airlines, but could improve reliability over the medium term. MetroAir shares fell 3.1% in intraday trade before recovering some losses.
author: ET Bureau
